speaker: interviewer
text: March 24th, 1995. The survivor is Anya Kornhauser and this is roll number three. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now you mentioned that after the war was over, you were allowed to return to Poland right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were-- after it was over, we were allowed to-- all Polish citizens were allowed to return to Poland legally, but we left Poland illegally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you-- when you left Poland you went to Czechoslovakia first.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Czechoslovakia as-- as Greek Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: As Greek Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a network of people from Palestine who was working with everybody. They told us what to do, what to say. And we weren't supposed to know any Polish. And if somebody talked to us, they said, just say a Hebrew word. If you don't know any Hebrew, just say anything, just don't speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't use your German passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no passport. We were going without passports, no passports.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that at-- at some point, you did get the Polish passport but that was later.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was-- when-- when we were in Poland, that's a short time. I don't I don't think we got a Polish passport though. We were displaced people. We had a paper that said-- we have a passport that said displaced person.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. But did you have to show this when you entered Czech territory? Or were you smuggled in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we weren't, like, smuggled in. We entered as Greek Jews returning from concentration camps. And only one of the people was allowed to know a little bit of German so he could communicate. See, the Polish soldiers on the border were telling the other one, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Czech.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Czech, it's the Polish captain. He said to them, they are no Greeks, those are Jews. A Pole could tell who-- who's Jewish. Those are Jews. They-- they understand what I'm saying, I can tell it by the way they look at me. But we didn't say a word. But one among us was allowed to know a few German words. So he kept to ask them in very-- very briefly in German, like, on his watch, what time can we leave? And he showed us, and we left. And even though the soldiers weren't too happy, they were ready to turn us in, but he said let them go. When we came to Czechoslovakia on a train, and again, we're not supposed to talk. So when he heard we were Greek Jews, he said-- they were very nice to us-- he said there's a Greek man here who speaks Greek. I'll go get him. Oh, that's bad because we can't speak the language. So as soon as he went away, we ran away because if he'll bring a Greek person, we can't speak any Greek. So we have to tell them who we are. But the network that was going from Palestine, they had a place for us where we should go. So we knew-- we came to a special room, to a special house, and there was food for-- there  were no people-- food for us, a bed to sleep in. And from there, we went to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from there to Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Vienna, by-- on the-- on a train.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, we weren't supposed to talk. And a Russian officer came on the train. And he said to us, I know who you are. Don't be afraid, I'm Jewish. You can talk to me. But you can talk to the wall. We never answered, not a word. And he spoke in Russian. He spoke in Yiddish. We didn't answer him. But he--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were afraid to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Afraid. We're afraid maybe he isn't who he says he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd been tricked too many times.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. So he left, and this train took us to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were traveling as displaced persons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, when we were traveling from there, from Poland, we were traveling as Greek citizens going from concentration camp back home to Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but when you-- when you went from Czechoslovakia to Vienna, did you have any kind of identification papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We had no papers at all. We were just going back home, wherever home is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had no problem crossing the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went on the train. These trains were there. So we just sit in the train sometimes a whole day and a whole night until the train would go. And when we came to Vienna, somehow there must have been one leader. So I don't know who. And they took us-- we called it a hotel. It is a hotel-- it wasn't a hotel, it was an empty hotel. So in Polish, we called it [SPEAKING POLISH] which means a poor hotel without money. It-- by translation, you-- you just-- it loses in its translation. But we were told not to leave. We never went out of there. We stayed a few days, and after a few days they took us to Wels.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was some kind of an underground organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a network to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Network, I understand. Zionist sponsored.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this Russian captain who-- who was on the train, was that some kind of border control or he just happened to be on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he had an idea probably of who we are. We never talked to him. We understood everything he said but we made believe we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he never-- he-- he-- he never-- you never confirmed your identity to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. And he said, you don't have to be afraid. I'm Jewish, too. But we didn't-- didn't trust anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you got to this hiding place or halfway house, whatever you want to call it, in Vienna, what happened after that? How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple weeks, I think. I don't remember exactly, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody come and--

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd come. They-- they used to give us portions, some food. I mean we weren't hungry but it was nothing special to talk about. And we used to sleep, like, 100 people in one room. They had cots there, little pillows, and the necessities of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So compared to what you had experienced earlier, this was good.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was good because we had hope. We knew we were going to something better. And when a person has hope, you have a lot. Because we were-- we didn't see Germans around us, that was good. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and then after this, you stayed in Vienna for--

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went to Wels, that was another DP camp. And well, there we were a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there we went to Bad Gastein, to the Alps. This was like paradise. The Austrian government put out all the-- the Austrian people, took away the hotels from them, and put us in there. And-- and you know, we had food there, and we had rooms, hotel rooms. These were better hotels than the one you stayed in before. That's quite a fancy [INTERPOSING VOICES] ski resort today.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] gorgeous. It was really beautiful there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how long did you stay in Bad Gastein?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bad Gastein, we stayed from-- until-- I came to Bad Gastein, it was the end of '45 and stayed there till I think, the beginning of '48. And from--

speaker: interviewer
text: That long.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so a lot of people, when they got papers to go to United States, would leave. I was waiting for papers but I didn't get them yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were going-- you were planning to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, by then already-- there was no Israel yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Palestine. In the meantime, I found somebody. My sister got married, and she went with her husband to Holland. And and from Holland-- she had a baby in Holland. And illegally, they went to Palestine. And when they came to Palestine, they didn't get through. And they-- the-- the British caught them. And they already walked the streets of Tel Aviv, and they shipped them back to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I get a letter from my sister saying never again am I going to Palestine. But three months later, I got a letter. They made it. See, they-- what they used to do when they came off a ship, the kibbutz-- you know what a kibbutz is-- they-- the kibbutz-- all the people in the kibbutz would come running to the ship and mix with the people. And if they didn't catch them, they brought all the people in. But my sister with the baby and her husband were caught and sent back to Holland. The first time.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time. And the second time, I was surprised to hear she went altogether. But the second time they made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did they go to Holland? Was he from Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He's from Austria. They were in Austria, in the same place I was. But this was like going on the way to Palestine they took you. You didn't ask questions. Whenever they took you, you went. It was a stop-over. And see she-- she was pregnant. She was gonna have a baby, so she stayed a little longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was not their decision. This was the network sent- told them to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was very well organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get on the ship from Rotterdam, perhaps. Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So meanwhile, you where still in Bad Gastein.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bad Gastein. Yes, in Bad Gastein, in the Alps. And then they finally gave the hotels back to the Austrians. So they sent us to Ebelsburg. That's near Linz.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the Danube.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So it wasn't as nice as Bad Gastein but it was livable. And-- and from there, we went-- that's when I-- I met my husband, actually before Bad Gastein. But that's when we got married in Austria then because my husband lived in Linz but in Bindermichl, Linz. And I lived in Ebelsburg in Linz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got married. We got married in [? Patsch. ?] [? Patsch ?] is a suburb of-- let's see-- not-- I forgot the name of the city already. Not Vienna, another big city, I can't remember. So we got married there, and then we lived in Linz in Bindermichl. And then I got some papers from some distant relatives to come to United States. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When you were at Ebelsburg, was that also a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a DP camp and where my husband was was a DP camp, too, in Bindermichl.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- these were very-- both very close to Linz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, those are suburbs of Linz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met-- actually met him at-- at Bad Gastein or at Ebelsburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him in Bad Gastein because I first met his friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took his friend to introduce him to me, and that's how it happened that I married my husband instead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Oh, I see. But you had-- you met him sometime earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I met him, must have been '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married in-- in-- Austria. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- in Austria in Ebelsburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so had you, by this time had you given up the thought of going to-- in '48 the state of Israel was in existence, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's when it was-- by that time, my brother and I-- this was before-- we gave up the idea of going to Palestine. We decided we've got papers from the United States. We'll go first to United States. We'll see what's doing there. We can aways go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- I forget, you mentioned you had relatives. Did your husband have relatives in the United States or-- or did you have relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had distant relatives. And my mother always had a girlfriend from many years ago. And I remembered her name, so I was looking for her. And the first thing she asked me, what zone I am, the Russian zone or the American zone because she wanted to send us papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you know her address in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked for her through the newspapers and I found her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish newspaper, It was called the

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Where did you get that, at the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well they used to come-- there were soldiers. American soldiers used to come to DP camp. So you gave them the information and when they would go home they would look in the papers. And it would put--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was not through the Red Cross or

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wasn't through the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some international organization? This was strictly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe some other people did through Red Cross, but we just did through-- through friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were able to get you-- your relatives in the United States were able to get you immigration visa?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got us papers and-- and we came here, my brother and my husband and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the United States? The United States, we came February 2nd, 1949. We got married in '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, 1949. So that was roughly almost four years after the war ended in Europe. So you had spent all that time in various DP camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: DP camps, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived-- where did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: On February 2nd?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, February 2nd, a lucky day.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's another lucky date.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we landed in New York, but the-- the family came to meet us there and they brought us to Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they met you at the ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a ship was that? What was that, a troop ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it wasn't a luxury ship. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No. How long did the trip take across the Atlantic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Too long. I don't remember how long, but I was so sick I didn't  care if I lived or died.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were happy to arrive in Boston.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. That's-- that was really a wonderful thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the end-- end of your journey, so to speak. Now, you mentioned earlier that you had-- you had saved the picture from your passport where you had straightened your hair. Do you have any other pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have other pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the war time or before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: From before, I only have-- I have my mother's picture, and I have my older brother's picture that we got someplace from somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's the one who was inducted into the Soviet Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never heard from him after all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. And the different people I saw in Israel-- I've been to Israel six times [INAUDIBLE] and I always look for him. And nobody ever saw him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever made many effort after-- after the fall of the Soviet-- the break up of the Soviet Union, have you ever made any effort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, through the Red Cross. The Red Cross-- as a matter of fact, I was working for the Red Cross. I was volunteering and I knew now that they-- they encourage you to give names. And a lot of people did find some, even if they're already not alive. But I said to them, to my friends at a Holocaust Center, I'll give you all the information. If my brother is alive, I'll go to the other side of the world to see him. But if they want to tell me how he died, I don't want to know now. [INAUDIBLE] I just don't think I could take it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. Now, when-- when you-- so you've been living in Boston?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a suburb of Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: A suburb of Boston. And what kind of work did your husband do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband had a-- a chain of coin-operated laundromats and dry cleaning, coin-operated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what have you occupied your time with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What have-- what-- what have you been--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was doing more-- I think I was doing the things my mother used to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Social, community work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yeah, a lot. I still do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me an example.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was President of Hadassa. I was various things. I was chairman of different things at a temple. I worked at the-- like, even now, I volunteer at the Jewish Journal in Peabody. I-- I work for the Holocaust Center. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Peabody, in Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you have a Holocaust Center there, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a Holocaust-- our Holocaust center is a nonsectarian one. And I speak in schools and churches and in universities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've been very active in Jewish Affairs and also helpful in keeping the memory of the Holocaust alive. And you mentioned a term [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] these people who helped you, they were Protestants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh... Okay. Protestant, and I was gonna say this. I think that's what you're referring to, that my topic is the Holocaust and the Righteous Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Righteous Gentiles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Righteous Gentiles. Although there were very few in numbers, but nevertheless, there were some. There were some that helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's wonderful that you have been-- been able to share these experiences with us. And your work in-- in keeping the memory of the Holocaust alive is certainly highly commendable. So on behalf of the Survivors of the Shoah Foundation, I want to thank you again for giving us of your rich memories. And perhaps we can take the remaining time and take a look at some of your family pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my mother, Perl Shapiro Kagan, taken in [POLISH], Poland approximately in 1930's. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my brother, Lejzer Kagan, who was drafted into the Russian Army in 1940, and I never saw him again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was drafted in-- This is a picture of me taken in the city of Rovno for a passport in 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of me taken in Bad Gastein in a DP camp in 1946. And my name is Anya Kornhauser. This is my husband, Jack Kornhauser, and it was taken in Bindermichl, Linz in Austria in 1947. This is a picture of me, Anya Kagan Kornhauser, with my husband, Jack Kornhauser, taken in Bad Gastein, Austria, in 1946, I think. This is a picture of me, Anya Kornhauser, and my husband, Jack Kornhauser, at a surprise birthday party on his 65th birthday, a year before he passed away in-- in Peabody, Massachusetts, USA. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my sister and her family and her husband and my brother and I taken in Israel in Petah Tikva in the '60s. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my son, Leon Kornhauser, and his wife, Bettina, taken in Molten, Massachusetts in [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] in-- 19--19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three years ago, yeah. I think 1972. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my daughter-in-law Bettina and my two grandchildren, Rachel and Michelle. Was taken in Boston, Massachusetts about two years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1992. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my son, Mark Kornhauser, with his wife, Susan, taken in Molten, Massachusetts in 1970-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in 1988. I knew I was gonna forget. This is my son-- my grandson, Adam Kornhauser, and taken in Maryland in 1993. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Laura Kornhauser, my granddaughter, Adam's sister and Mark's daughter. It was taken in Maryland in 1993. Three? I'm looking above there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Jack Kornhauser. The picture was taken in Linz, Austria in 1947.
